---
# ── FILL IN YOUR DETAILS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
title: "Your Post Title"
author: "Your Name"
date: "2025-03-01"
description: "One sentence. Make it good."
categories: [social psychology]
image: "images/cover.jpg"
stream: publication
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

bibliography: references.bib
toc: false
---

<!-- DELETE THIS BLOCK before submitting ──────────────────────────────────────
STREAM: PUBLICATION

This template is for a general-audience piece. Think The Conversation, Psyche,
or New Scientist. Aim for 800–1200 words. No jargon without explanation.
Strong hook. Clear narrative. Citations exist but don't interrupt flow.

You MUST include:
  - TL;DR box (below the image)
  - At least one pull quote
  - At least one tabset
  - A share link at the bottom

Suggested structure:
  1. Cover image with lede caption
  2. TL;DR box
  3. Opening paragraphs (hook + stakes)
  4. Main argument in tabset sections
  5. Pull quote
  6. Closing section
  7. Share link + author card

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── -->


<!-- Cover image. Find something on Unsplash (free, no attribution needed for web). -->
![Replace this with a compelling image related to your topic.
*(Photo: [Unsplash](https://unsplash.com))*](images/cover.jpg)


::: {.tldr}
- First key point — the surprising finding or claim
- Second key point — why it matters or what the evidence shows
- Third key point — the takeaway or implication
:::


## Your opening hook

Don't start with "In this essay..." Start with something that makes the reader want to keep going. A scene. A statistic that surprises them. A question they hadn't thought to ask.

Give the stakes here. Why should a non-psychologist care about this? What does this topic connect to in real life?


::: {.panel-tabset}

## The research

Explain the science here in plain language. What do studies actually show?
You can cite in-line [@authorYear] but keep citations light — one or two is enough.

## What it means

The so-what. Connect the research to something the reader can feel or apply.
Concrete examples work better than abstractions here.

## The honest caveats

What doesn't the research tell us? What should readers keep in mind?
This builds trust. Readers respect honesty about limitations.

:::


::: {.pullquote}
"Pull out one sentence from your post that captures the whole argument.
Make it quotable. This is the line that might get screenshot and shared."
:::


## Closing section

Bring it home. One clear paragraph. Don't trail off or hedge — land the point.
What do you want the reader to think, feel, or do after reading this?


---

*If this post made you think, you can [share it on Bluesky](https://bsky.app/intent/compose?text=Interesting+read+from+%40ps50blog%3A+PASTE-YOUR-URL-HERE).*

---

::: {.author-card}

<div>
<div class="author-card-name">Your Name</div>
<div class="author-card-bio">
  Psychology student at Goldsmiths, University of London.
  One sentence about your interests.
</div>
</div>
:::

*This post is published under [CC BY 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/).*


## References

::: {#refs}
:::
